{"step_by_step_thinking": "Cross-sectional studies provide a snapshot of the characteristics of a population at a single point in time, making them quick to conduct. However, they cannot establish causation. Case-control studies are also quick to conduct and can establish a correlation between exposures and outcomes. Randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for establishing causation but are not always feasible for studying the association between smoking and lung cancer due to ethical concerns and practicality. 'Coho study' is not a recognized term in medical literature. Given the need to quickly assess the strength of association, a case-control study is the best option.", "answer_choice": "B"}
